raw_case:
Intraoperative diagnosis of Amyand's hernia, a case report


Introduction

Hernia forms when an organ or its fascia protrudes through the continuing cavity's wall. Amyand's hernia describes an inguinal hernia containing an appendix, regardless of inflammation.

Presentation of case

Our patient was complaining of an increasing lump in the inguinal area. It started 5 years ago as a small mass that gradually increased, manifesting distressing symptoms. The initial diagnosis was an indirect right inguinal hernia. During the surgery, the presence of an indirect inguinal hernia was observed, and the appendix was revealed within the sac, with the observation of adhesion of the appendix tip to the sac.

Discussion

Amyand's hernia describes an inguinal hernia, containing the appendix inside it. It is classified according to Losanoff and Basson depending on the presence and spread of inflammation. The diagnosis of Amyand's hernia is often made during the surgery because of its rarity and unspecific symptoms. When symptoms and signs occur, they masquerade as strangulated hernia rather than appendicitis.

Conclusion

Even Amyand's hernia is a rare condition; it should be a differential diagnosis in patients who come with strangulated hernia or appendicitis. More studies are needed to understand the physiology of this type of hernia. In addition, more cases should be reported to help establish guidelines for diagnosing and treating this hernia.

Highlights

•Even Amyand's hernia is a rare condition; it should be a differential diagnosis.•More studies are needed to understand the physiology of this type of hernia.•More cases should be reported to help put guidelines in place for diagnosis and treatment.1

Introduction

Hernia forms when an organ or its fascia protrudes through the continuing cavity's wall [1]. Inguinal hernias are a common type, occurring in 27–43 % of men and 3–6 % of women [2]. Amyand's hernia (AH) describes an inguinal hernia containing an appendix inside it, regardless of inflammation [[2], [3], [4]]. Claudius Amyand is the first surgeon who described and treated it in 1735 [5]. It is a rare type, representing only 1 % of all inguinal hernias [3]. It is more common in males (90 % of patients), and on the right side because of the normal location of the appendix and the increased inguinal hernia in the right side [[2], [3], [4]]. This hernia is often asymptomatic; in some cases, it presents as incarcerated or strangulated hernia or acute appendicitis [6]. Therefore, it is difficult to diagnose it before surgery [2]. In this paper, we present a rare case of AH to share our clinical insights and encourage more studies about this unique condition. This work has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria [7].2

Case report

A 76-year-old male presented to our hospital, complaining of an increasing lump in the right inguinal area. It started 5 years ago as a small mass that gradually increased in size, manifesting distressing symptoms including sharp pain, along with sensations of heaviness and pressure in the inguinal area, exacerbated during prolonged physical activity. The patient's surgical history revealed a left inguinal hernia repair. Medical, familial, drug, and psychosocial histories were unremarkable. Clinical evaluation revealed a large reversible inguinal hernia protruding notably during standing, coughing, increased abdominal pressure, and reduced in a recumbent position. The physical examination of other systems was within normal. The diagnosis depending on clinical manifestations and examination was indirect right inguinal hernia. The patient underwent laboratory tests and consultations before surgery, and they were all within normal. During the surgery, which was done by a specialist, the presence of an indirect inguinal hernia sac was observed coursing alongside the spermatic cord. It was isolated from the cord and opened for examination of its contents, revealing the appendix within the sac, with observation of adhesion of the appendix tip to the inner wall of the sac (Fig. 1, Fig. 2, Fig. 3). Adhesiolysis of the appendix was performed, followed by its excision, ligature of the hernia sac, and subsequent excision, along with concurrent repair of the inguinal hernia utilizing the Lichtenstein technique. The patient was discharged from the hospital after 2 days without any complications. The follow-up for six months was within normal.3

Discussion

AH describes an inguinal hernia, containing the appendix inside it, regardless of the inflammation [4]. It is classified according to Losanoff and Basson depending on the presence and spread of inflammation (Table 1) [6]. Many theories try to explain the pathology of AH, one of them suggests that it may be a congenital situation as there are studies that describe a fibrous connection between the right testis and appendix with the presence of patent processus vaginalis, this connection can lead to the appendix towards the hernia sac; the presence of this hernia in the neonates and premature twins supports this theory. Left AH has been described in patients with intestine malrotation, situs inversus, and mobile cecum [4]. Many causes explain appendicitis in AH including (1) the extraluminal obstruction due to the pressure on the hernia neck, the abdominal muscles' contraction, and any agent can increase the intraabdominal pressure; which leads to cut off the blood supply and increase the bacterial growth later, (2) vulnerability to trauma during migration (3) adhesions form between the appendix and internal sac wall, resulting in forming irreducible hernia, which is prone to external trauma [1,2,8]. The diagnosis of AH is often made during the surgery because of its rarity and unspecific symptoms [9]. When symptoms and signs occur, they masquerade as strangulated hernia rather than appendicitis, including groin pain, nausea and vomiting, irreducibility, and tenderness [6]. In acute cases, the patient complaints of sudden-onset pain in the epigastrium or periumbilical region with tenderness in the right lower quadrant, combined with tender irreducible mass in the inguinal or inguinoscrotal region [1]. In addition to AH, many differential diagnoses should be in our minds such as irreducible, incarcerated or strangulated hernia, acute appendicitis, strangulated omentocele, testicular tumor with hemorrhage, torsion of the testis, Richter's hernia, inguinal adenitis, acute hydrocele, and epididymitis [2,8]. Even the final diagnosis is made during surgery; many methods may help us such as laboratory tests (elevated white blood cells and CRP differentiate inflamed appendix from healthy one), ultrasound imaging (shows non-compressible, blind-ended, cecum-linked tubular structure in the hernia sac), and computed tomography (CT) scanning, which is considered the preferred method; it allows us to see the appendix directly in hernia type 1, but in types 2 and 3, we Can see thickened appendix, fat stranding, fluid collections or abscess because of the inflammation process [2,9]. Diagnostic laparoscopy is also recommended as a diagnostic method. The management of AH is depending on its classification (Table 1) [3]. The appendectomy in type 1 hernias is still controversial, some surgeons support routine appendectomy, while others refuse it as many researches show that removing the appendix may affect the immune system development in children. The treatment of Left-sided type 1 AH contains appendectomy to avoid future diagnostic problems [2]. In our case, we observed during the surgery that the tip of the appendix linked into the inner wall of the sac, which supports the presence of an inflammatory process, so an appendectomy was done. The complications of AH include periappendicular or appendicular abscess, appendicular perforation, intra-abdominal abscess, necrotizing fasciitis of the anterior abdominal wall, scrotal fistula, and local thrombosis rarely [4].4

Conclusion

AH should be in our minds as differential diagnosis in patients come with strangulated hernia, appendicitis, and acute abdomen. Even the complications of this type of hernia are rare, but they are dangerous and life-threatening. Therefore, fast and accurate diagnosis and treatment are essential. More studies are needed to understand the physiology of this rare type of hernia and to determine the best diagnosis and treatment methods.

Consent for publication

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal.

Ethical approval

Not applicable because the article does not include any personal information.

Funding

There are no sources of funding.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 76-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Increasing lump in the right inguinal area
- **History of Present Illness:** The lump started 5 years ago as a small mass that gradually increased in size, accompanied by sharp pain, sensations of heaviness, and pressure in the inguinal area, exacerbated by prolonged physical activity.
- **Past Medical History:** Previous left inguinal hernia repair
- **Personal History:** None
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** Large reversible right inguinal hernia protruding during standing, coughing, and increased abdominal pressure; reducible in a recumbent position. No abnormal findings in other systems.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Routine laboratory tests were normal. Imaging tests were not mentioned. Intraoperative examination revealed an indirect inguinal hernia sac containing an appendix, with its tip adherent to the sac wall.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial consideration: Indirect right inguinal hernia**  
   - Clinical presentation (lump in the inguinal region) was typical of an inguinal hernia. Initial diagnosis was made based on physical examination.

2. **Consideration of Amyand's hernia**  
   - During surgery, the presence of the appendix inside the hernia sac, with adhesions to the sac wall, confirmed the diagnosis of Amyand's hernia. This unusual finding prompted reclassification from a simple inguinal hernia to Amyand's hernia.

3. **Exclusion of other differential diagnoses:**
   - **Strangulated hernia:** No evidence of strangulation (normal appendicular appearance with no ischemic changes).
   - **Appendicitis:** Despite adhesions, there was no acute inflammation or signs of appendicitis (normal laboratory tests and intraoperative findings).
   - **Testicular or scrotal pathologies (e.g., torsion, tumor):** No scrotal abnormalities were noted on physical examination.

Rationale: Amyand's hernia is rare and typically diagnosed intraoperatively due to its non-specific preoperative presentation. Surgical findings, including the presence of the appendix within the sac, confirmed the diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Indirect right inguinal hernia containing the appendix (Amyand's hernia, Type 1).

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient is a 76-year-old male presenting with an increasing lump in the right inguinal area for 5 years. The gradual increase in size, accompanied by pain, heaviness, pressure, and exacerbation with physical activity strongly suggests an inguinal hernia. The duration of 5 years indicates a chronic condition, but the increasing size implies ongoing progression.

<step 2> **Evaluate the Past Medical History:**  A previous left inguinal hernia repair is significant. It indicates a predisposition to inguinal hernias in this patient, increasing the likelihood that the current right inguinal lump is also a hernia.  It also suggests a potential underlying weakness in the inguinal region.

<step 3> **Interpret the Physical Examination Findings:** The physical exam confirms the presence of a large, reversible right inguinal hernia that protrudes with increased abdominal pressure and reduces in a recumbent position.  This is classic for a reducible inguinal hernia. The description "large" indicates a significant size, consistent with the 5-year history of growth.

<step 4> **Consider Ancillary Tests (and Lack Thereof):** Routine lab tests being normal are less relevant to the diagnosis of a mechanical issue like a hernia, but they help rule out systemic inflammatory processes. The absence of imaging before surgery is not unusual for clinically obvious inguinal hernias, especially if uncomplicated.

<step 5> **Integrate the Intraoperative Findings:** The intraoperative finding of an indirect inguinal hernia sac containing the appendix, with its tip adherent to the sac wall, is the definitive diagnostic information. This finding not only confirms the diagnosis of an inguinal hernia but also specifies it as containing the appendix, classifying it as a rare type of inguinal hernia known as an Amyand's hernia. The "indirect" type further specifies the anatomical pathway of the hernia, through the internal inguinal ring. The adhesion suggests chronicity and potential for inflammation or complications if left untreated.

<step 6> **Formulate the Final Diagnosis:**  Based on the clinical presentation, physical examination, and crucially, the intraoperative findings, the final diagnosis is an indirect inguinal hernia, specifically containing the appendix within the hernia sac. This specific type of hernia, containing the appendix, is known as an Amyand's hernia. Therefore, the most precise diagnosis is an Amyand's hernia, right inguinal, indirect.  However, simply stating "Indirect Inguinal Hernia with Appendix in Sac" is also accurate and clinically relevant based on the provided information.  Given the provided information and common clinical practice,  "Indirect Inguinal Hernia with Appendix in Sac" is a sufficiently detailed and accurate diagnosis.

### Answer:
Indirect Inguinal Hernia with Appendix in Sac

reasoning_eval.step: **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient is a 76-year-old male presenting with an increasing lump in the right inguinal area for 5 years. The gradual increase in size, accompanied by pain, heaviness, pressure, and exacerbation with physical activity strongly suggests an inguinal hernia. The duration of 5 years indicates a chronic condition, but the increasing size implies ongoing progression.

**Evaluate the Past Medical History:**  A previous left inguinal hernia repair is significant. It indicates a predisposition to inguinal hernias in this patient, increasing the likelihood that the current right inguinal lump is also a hernia.  It also suggests a potential underlying weakness in the inguinal region.

**Interpret the Physical Examination Findings:** The physical exam confirms the presence of a large, reversible right inguinal hernia that protrudes with increased abdominal pressure and reduces in a recumbent position.  This is classic for a reducible inguinal hernia. The description "large" indicates a significant size, consistent with the 5-year history of growth.

**Consider Ancillary Tests (and Lack Thereof):** Routine lab tests being normal are less relevant to the diagnosis of a mechanical issue like a hernia, but they help rule out systemic inflammatory processes. The absence of imaging before surgery is not unusual for clinically obvious inguinal hernias, especially if uncomplicated.

**Integrate the Intraoperative Findings:** The intraoperative finding of an indirect inguinal hernia sac containing the appendix, with its tip adherent to the sac wall, is the definitive diagnostic information. This finding not only confirms the diagnosis of an inguinal hernia but also specifies it as containing the appendix, classifying it as a rare type of inguinal hernia known as an Amyand's hernia. The "indirect" type further specifies the anatomical pathway of the hernia, through the internal inguinal ring. The adhesion suggests chronicity and potential for inflammation or complications if left untreated.

**Formulate the Final Diagnosis:**  Based on the clinical presentation, physical examination, and crucially, the intraoperative findings, the final diagnosis is an indirect inguinal hernia, specifically containing the appendix within the hernia sac. This specific type of hernia, containing the appendix, is known as an Amyand's hernia. Therefore, the most precise diagnosis is an Amyand's hernia, right inguinal, indirect.  However, simply stating "Indirect Inguinal Hernia with Appendix in Sac" is also accurate and clinically relevant based on the provided information.  Given the provided information and common clinical practice,  "Indirect Inguinal Hernia with Appendix in Sac" is a sufficiently detailed and accurate diagnosis.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial consideration: Indirect right inguinal hernia
Clinical presentation (lump in the inguinal region) was typical of an inguinal hernia; initial diagnosis made based on physical examination.
During surgery, presence of the appendix inside the hernia sac with adhesions to the sac wall confirmed Amyand's hernia; reclassified from a simple inguinal hernia to Amyand's hernia.

No evidence of strangulation (normal appendicular appearance with no ischemic changes); no acute inflammation or signs of appendicitis (normal laboratory tests and intraoperative findings); no scrotal abnormalities noted on physical examination.
Rationale: Amyand's hernia is rare and typically diagnosed intraoperatively due to its non-specific preoperative presentation; surgical findings confirmed the diagnosis.
Indirect right inguinal hernia containing the appendix (Amyand's hernia, Type 1).
